message.content: Adversaries may achieve persistence on Unix-based systems by modifying shell configuration scripts that are executed during various user session events. These scripts, such as `/etc/profile`, `~/.profile`, and `~/.bashrc`, are used to set environment variables and customize the user environment. By inserting malicious commands into these scripts, adversaries can ensure their code runs whenever a user opens a command-line interface or logs in remotely. This technique requires root permissions for system-wide scripts but can also be executed at the user level for individual user scripts. Common exploitation methods include adding commands to launch malicious binaries or scripts, ensuring execution by targeting the first existing file in the script order. On macOS, similar persistence can be achieved using zsh configuration files like `/etc/zprofile` and `~/.zlogin`. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting modifications to shell configuration files, employing file integrity monitoring, and using access controls to limit script modifications to authorized users only. Regular audits and anomaly detection can also help identify unauthorized changes to these critical files.
